title: Dog Walking Uncoordinated and Stumbling: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is stumbling or uncoordinated, it could be a serious emergency. Learn about ataxia, when to see a vet, and how a video can help.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ataxia is a loss of coordination causing a drunken-like gait and is considered a high-priority veterinary emergency requiring immediate evaluation.

What is ataxia in dogs and what does it look like?

Ataxia is the medical term for when a dog begins walking as if they are intoxicated, stumbling, or wobbling. This loss of coordination occurs when the brain, spinal cord, or inner ear is not communicating correctly with the body. It can manifest as:

  • A swaying or wobbly gait
  • Tilting the head to one side
  • The legs crossing over one another while walking

Is it an emergency if my dog is stumbling or walking like they are drunk?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, a sudden onset of uncoordinated movement is considered a veterinary emergency. While some causes like ear infections are less severe, others can be life-threatening if not treated immediately. Seek emergency care right away if your dog:

  • Cannot stand or walk on their own
  • Has rhythmic, involuntary eye movements
  • Appears visibly distressed or disoriented
  • Has potentially ingested a toxin or suffered a stroke or spinal injury

Why should I take a video of my dog's uncoordinated movements for the vet?

When you contact a vet, providing a short video of your dog attempting to walk can be invaluable. A video helps the triage process because:

  • Symptoms of ataxia can be intermittent or may disappear due to the adrenaline and stress of being at a clinic.
  • It allows the veterinarian to analyze the specific pattern of the movement.
  • It helps the doctor differentiate between neurological issues, orthopedic pain, or vestibular disease before the physical exam begins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Uncoordinated walking and stumbling in dogs can stem from various neurological issues. Cerebellar disorders often manifest as an intention tremor, wide-based stance, and incoordination (dysmetria) of the head, trunk, and limbs. Brainstem disorders may lead to cranial nerve dysfunction, postural disturbances, or vestibular dysfunction, potentially causing weakness and proprioceptive gait disturbances; severely affected animals may also exhibit impaired consciousness. Spinal cord disorders can cause weakness, motor dysfunction, or proprioceptive deficits in the limbs, including dysmetria or reduced proprioceptive placing in multiple limbs, without affecting cerebral function or head coordination. Subtle gait deficits can be accentuated by observing the animal walking up and down a grade or while blindfolded; more subtle deficits may be detected by postural reaction testing, such as conscious proprioceptive positioning where the animal should immediately replace a displaced foot to a normal position. Vestibular dysfunction may cause ipsilateral falling, rolling, or circling.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1217)

faqs.question: Can my dog's stumbling be caused by something they ate?
faqs.answer: Yes, ingestion of certain toxins, such as marijuana, xylitol, or certain human medications, can cause a dog to lose coordination and stumble.

faqs.question: Is stumbling just a normal sign of aging?
faqs.answer: While senior dogs may slow down due to arthritis, sudden uncoordinated movement or 'drunken' walking is never normal and indicates a medical issue rather than simple aging.

faqs.question: What should I do while waiting to get to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog confined to a small, safe area with no stairs or sharp corners to prevent them from falling and injuring themselves further.
